summary: Read-only map of the current MRR AI Flask app into a Next.js + FastAPI porting spec

result.summary: The app exposes 59 blueprint routes across two disjoint surfaces plus Flask-Security's auto-registered auth endpoints. The MODERN account flow is DB-backed and per-user: documents_api (15 routes under /api/documents, every handler owner-scoped via _own() -> 404-on-miss IDOR guard) and admin_api (7 routes under /api/admin, gated by is_admin), plus a handful of pages.py routes that render SPA shells (/, /review/<id>, /diagnostics, /depositions, /admin). These map cleanly onto FastAPI routers + Pydantic models + auth dependencies (rewrite). The CLASSIC single-user flow is state.py-global-based and multi-user-broken: review_api (5 routes: /api/pdf, /api/segment/*, /api/summarize/*), upload (3), summarize (2, OpenAI), reports (3), extraction (2), individual_mrr (2), export (3), segmentation (2), and the classic template pages + /reset in pages.py. Despite its name and the task briefing grouping it as modern, review_api is fully state.py-based and no front-end references it, so it drops with the rest of the classic paths. Auth is a single app-level before_request deny-by-default gate in security.py (not per-route decorators), with admin path-prefix escalation; CSRF is global CSRFProtect using an XSRF-TOKEN cookie echoed as an X-XSRF-Token header on the fetch APIs. The two job-enqueuing modern routes (segment/start, summarize/start, admin reprocess) submit to services/job_queue and are the seam where the endpoint stays a thin POST handler but the underlying pipeline is redesigned onto Redis + workers; resummarize and bundle/summarize run synchronously in-request today.

files.role: Blueprint registration order; register_blueprints(app) wires all 11 blueprints
App-level deny-by-default auth gate (before_request), PUBLIC_ENDPOINTS, ADMIN_PATH_PREFIXES, CSRF + Flask-Security wiring, password rules
App factory: config (session cookie, CSRF cookie/header, SECURITY_* flags), schema, job_queue.init_app
Module-global mutable state backing the CLASSIC single-user flow (drop target)
MODERN per-user document API (15 routes, /api/documents), _own() IDOR guard, job targets
MODERN admin API (7 routes, /api/admin), is_admin-gated category/prompt CRUD + reprocess
CLASSIC state-based review API (5 routes /api/*); no front-end consumer -> drop
Template-render + redirect routes (15); mix of MODERN SPA shells (rewrite) and CLASSIC pages + /reset (drop)
CLASSIC state-based upload routes (3) -> drop
CLASSIC OpenAI summarize routes (2), state-based, writes all_data_temp.txt -> drop
CLASSIC diag/depo extraction + page-range merge (3), state-based, writes ~/MRRs -> drop
CLASSIC OpenAI name/DOB/lawfirm extraction (2), state-based -> drop
CLASSIC patient-folder + multi-file upload (2), state-based -> drop
CLASSIC export routes (3) -> drop; _build_mrr_document + _DOCX_MIMETYPE are framework-agnostic docx helpers reused by documents_api -> port-as-is as a service
CLASSIC segmentPDF + getPages (2), state-based -> drop
db + SQLite WAL pragmas + genai/OpenAI clients; not routes but shapes DB/session concurrency for the port

items.name: POST /login (Flask-Security)
items.detail: security.py:75 Security(app,...); auto-registered by Flask-Security. In PUBLIC_ENDPOINTS (security.login). Content-negotiated: HTML form (WTForms csrf_token) vs JSON 401. Session cookie (SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY, SameSite=Lax; SECURE deferred to HTTPS). Not a blueprint route. Rewrite as FastAPI auth (session/token).

items.name: POST /register (Flask-Security)
items.detail: Auto-registered; SECURITY_REGISTERABLE=True, CONFIRMABLE=False, no register email (no SMTP). MrrRegisterForm adds required 'name'; MrrPasswordUtil enforces 8+ / digit / symbol server-side. In PUBLIC_ENDPOINTS (security.register). Rewrite as FastAPI signup + Pydantic validators.

items.name: POST /logout (Flask-Security)
items.detail: Auto-registered; SECURITY_POST_LOGOUT_VIEW='/login'. Invalidates session (and CSRF tokens). NOT in PUBLIC_ENDPOINTS -> requires session. Rewrite as FastAPI logout.

items.name: before_request deny-by-default gate
items.detail: security.py:82 _require_authentication. App-level (not per-route): endpoint None -> 404; endpoint in PUBLIC_ENDPOINTS{security.login,security.register,static} -> allow; else require current_user.is_authenticated (else login_manager.unauthorized -> redirect or 401 JSON); if path under /admin or /api/admin and not is_admin -> abort 403. Rewrite as FastAPI dependencies (get_current_user + require_admin) applied at router level.

items.name: CSRF protection (global CSRFProtect)
items.detail: security.py:69 CSRFProtect(app) before Security. Unsafe methods require X-XSRF-Token header echoed from XSRF-TOKEN cookie (SECURITY_CSRF_COOKIE_NAME) for fetch APIs; WTForms hidden csrf_token for login/register; WTF_CSRF_TIME_LIMIT=None. Rewrite/redesign: FastAPI has no built-in CSRF; token-auth SPA model changes this (double-submit or bearer-token instead).

portingNotes: Two FastAPI routers cover the entire keep-set: documents router (prefix /api/documents, 15 routes) and admin router (prefix /api/admin, 7 routes). Everything else is either dropped (classic) or becomes Next.js pages/routes (the pages.py SPA shells).

Auth: replace the single before_request gate with FastAPI dependencies. A get_current_user dependency on both routers reproduces deny-by-default; a require_admin dependency on the admin router (and any /admin page) reproduces the is_admin path-prefix escalation. Do NOT port as per-route decorators piecemeal - the original design deliberately gates at app level so new routes are protected by default; mirror that with router-level dependencies.

IDOR guard: _own(document_id) = Document.query.filter_by(id=..., user_id=current_user.id).first() returning 404 (not 403) on miss. Reproduce as a shared FastAPI dependency get_owned_document(document_id, user) that raises HTTPException(404); every documents route must depend on it.

Pydantic request models: RowsPayload {rows:[RowModel{start:int,end:int,category:str,title,date,injury_date,flag,suggest_merge:bool,include:bool=True}]}; SummarizeStartPayload {rows?:[RowModel], model?:str}; SummaryEditPayload {summaryTitle?,summaryDate?,summaryText?,excluded?}; ExportPayload {patientName,patientdob,QMEorAME,lawfirm}; BundlePayload {categories:list[str], label?:str, model?:str} + export fields; CategoryCreate/Update and PromptPut for admin. validate_rows (review_api.py:18) is framework-agnostic domain logic -> port-as-is into a shared validator used by the Pydantic model / service.

File responses: get_pdf and all export/bundle downloads use Flask send_file -> FastAPI FileResponse (disk) or StreamingResponse(BytesIO) with Content-Disposition. Multipart upload (create_document) -> FastAPI UploadFile parameter named 'pdf'.

Job pipeline (redesign): segment/start, summarize/start, and admin reprocess call services/job_queue.submit with a per-request closure target (_segment_target/_summarize_target that mutate DB inside an app context). For Redis + external workers these closures cannot be pickled - the enqueue must send a serializable job descriptor (document_id, kind, model, prompt_version, catalog_revision) and the worker reconstructs the target. status/get_status polling reads Job.progress() from DB today; keep the DB Job row as the progress source of truth so the FastAPI status endpoint stays a simple DB read regardless of the queue backend.

Synchronous AI routes (resummarize, bundle/summarize) run model calls in-request today; in FastAPI these should be async def with the blocking OCR/model work offloaded (run_in_threadpool or a short queue job) to avoid blocking the event loop - a behavioral porting decision, not a queue redesign.

CSRF: the XSRF-TOKEN cookie / X-XSRF-Token header contract disappears with FastAPI. Decide the SPA auth model (session cookie + double-submit CSRF, or bearer token in Authorization header) up front because it changes every mutating fetch in the Next.js client.

Shared helper _build_mrr_document + _DOCX_MIMETYPE (export.py:19) and _export_entry/_PAGES_SUFFIX + _download_name (documents_api.py) are pure python-docx/regex with no Flask coupling at the function body -> port-as-is into a services/reporting module reused by the documents router.

catalog.get_category_options / get_category_ids / get_prompt / bump_revision / catalog_version and services.audit.audit are framework-agnostic and already sit behind the endpoints -> port-as-is; the endpoints just call them.
risks: Task-briefing vs reality mismatch: the brief groups review_api into the MODERN account flow, but review_api.py is entirely state.py-global-based (state.pdf_filepath, state.all_data) on the single-slot services/jobs.py runner, and grep confirms NO template or front-end JS references /api/pdf, /api/segment/*, or /api/summarize/*. If it is dropped on that assumption, confirm no external/automation client hits those paths first.

Dropping the classic flow removes ALL /upload, /summarize, /getDiagOpRep, /getDepoRep, /compute_page_ranges, /getpatientnameanddob, /getlawfirm, individual-MRR, and classic export routes. If any real user workflow still runs through the /classic hub (index.html) rather than /, that capability must be re-created in the modern per-document flow before drop, or data/workflow is lost.

Job closures (_segment_target/_summarize_target) capture request-scoped values and mutate the DB inside a Flask app context; naive port to Redis workers will break because closures are not serializable and the worker has no Flask app context. Redesign must supply a worker-side app/DB context and a serializable descriptor.

Owner-scoping is enforced ONLY in documents_api via _own; admin reprocess intentionally bypasses it (db.session.get over any owner). The FastAPI port must preserve exactly this asymmetry (owner-checked user routes, is_admin-only cross-owner reprocess) or it introduces either an IDOR or a broken admin feature.

409-on-active-job concurrency guards (delete, put_rows, summarize_start, put_summary, resummarize) depend on document.active_job derived from the single-process job model; with an external queue the active_job check must be recomputed from the shared Job table/Redis, or two workers can race a document.

Several classic routes write PHI-bearing artifacts to ~/MRRs and all_data_temp.txt on local disk (export.py, reports.py, summarize.py); these are exactly the paths being dropped, but any migration/parity testing must not resurrect that local-disk PHI behavior in the new stack.
openQuestions: Confirm review_api (/api/pdf, /api/segment/*, /api/summarize/*) is truly dead: is the /classic + /pdfsegment UI still used by anyone, or can it be dropped outright with the rest of the state.py flow?

Should the modern flow gain feature parity for anything only the classic flow does today (e.g. the CSV-checker /uploadAndCheckCSV, multi-file individual-MRR folders, the aggregated-records PDF merge in /compute_page_ranges), or are those intentionally retired?
Auth model for the Next.js + FastAPI split: keep server session cookies (needs CSRF strategy) or move to bearer tokens? This decides how login/logout/register and every mutating endpoint are ported.

Flask-Security currently owns /login /logout /register plus password policy (MrrPasswordUtil: 8+/digit/symbol) and the required display-name field (MrrRegisterForm). Which library replaces this in FastAPI (fastapi-users, Authlib, custom) and does it need to keep reading the SAME user/role tables in the shared SQLite DB?

Job progress contract: is Job.progress() (DB-backed) the intended single source of truth for the status endpoints post-redesign, or will Redis carry live progress and the DB only final state? Affects whether get_status stays a plain DB read.

Should the two synchronous AI routes (resummarize, bundle/summarize) also move onto the external queue for consistency, or remain in-request (async) given they are bounded single-record / capped-bundle operations?

result.summary: The persistence layer is a single flask_sqlalchemy `db` (extensions.py:21) with 11 declarative models in models.py. Two of them (Role, User) are Flask-Security mixin models (fsqla_v3); the other nine (Document, Job, SegmentRow, ReviewRow, Summary, Category, Prompt, CatalogMeta, AuditLog) are plain SQLAlchemy and are framework-agnostic. Core domain shape: a Document owns Jobs (a Job is also the provenance record: model + prompt_version + catalog_revision), a Job owns immutable SegmentRow training INPUTs, a Document owns mutable ReviewRow training LABELs and Summary rows (raw title/date/text immutable + edited_* overrides + source_text = fine-tuning input half). Key invariants: Document.status has a single writer (the job service) with at most one queued/running job (active_job); SegmentRow-vs-ReviewRow is the training-data flywheel; Category ids are immutable strings with an active/auto_assign two-set distinction and soft-delete; CatalogMeta is a single-row (id=1) monotonic revision counter stamped onto jobs. SQLite is configured via a per-connection PRAGMA event listener (WAL + busy_timeout=5000 + foreign_keys=ON) so background job threads write while request threads read. There is NO Alembic: schema is created on boot via db.create_all(), patched by a hand-rolled additive ADD COLUMN loop (_ADDITIVE_COLUMNS), then the catalog tables are seed-if-empty from the taxonomy.py/prompts.py constants. The models port to FastAPI + SQLAlchemy essentially unchanged; the Flask-Security auth models and the boot create_all/ALTER/seed mechanism must be re-implemented (Alembic + FastAPI lifespan). The existing SQLite DB must be reused byte-compatibly, so the new declarative models must mirror the exact columns (especially the fsqla_v3 user/role schema) and the same PRAGMAs must be applied on every engine connection.

files.role: All 11 ORM models (users, documents, jobs, segment/review rows, summaries, catalog, audit) + status/kind enums + ROW_FIELDS
db = SQLAlchemy() singleton + SQLite PRAGMA connect event listener (WAL/busy_timeout/foreign_keys)
App factory: _create_schema boot create_all + _ADDITIVE_COLUMNS additive-ALTER migration + seed_catalog call
Seed-if-empty of Category/Prompt/CatalogMeta from constants + constants_* runtime fallbacks
DB-first catalog accessor (get_categories/get_prompt) + catalog_version/bump_revision counter
SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI default (sqlite:///instance/mrr.db), PIPELINE_WORKERS, env fail-fast
CATEGORIES constant (ids 1-5,7-14,100; NO id 6) - seed source + runtime fallback for categories
prompts dict (category_NN keys) - seed source + fallback for per-category summary prompts

items.name: Role model (table `role`)
items.detail: models.py:23-24. `class Role(db.Model, fsqla_v3.FsRoleMixin)` - empty body; all columns come from the Flask-Security fsqla_v3 mixin (id, name, description, permissions, update_datetime) plus the implicit roles_users association table. Table name defaults to `role`. Immutable auth schema.

items.name: User model (table `user`)
items.detail: models.py:27-35. `class User(db.Model, fsqla_v3.FsUserMixin)` + two app-added columns: `name` VARCHAR(255) nullable (registration-enforced, DB-nullable for boot migration) and `is_admin` BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT 0 (single-boolean admin gate, not RBAC). All other columns (id, email, password, active, fs_uniquifier, confirmed_at, login tracking, etc.) come from fsqla_v3 FsUserMixin - exact set is defined by the Flask-Security version, NOT enumerated in app code. FK targets `user.id` are used by Document.user_id and AuditLog.user_id.

items.name: Document model (table `documents`)
items.detail: models.py:58-97. Cols: id VARCHAR(36) PK (uuid4 default), user_id INT FK->user.id NOT NULL indexed, original_filename VARCHAR(512) NOT NULL (PHI-bearing, never log), stored_path VARCHAR(1024), sha256 VARCHAR(64) indexed, page_count INT, status VARCHAR(16) default 'uploaded', created_at/updated_at DateTime (onupdate). DOCUMENT_STATUSES=(uploaded,segmenting,reviewing,summarizing,done,error,interrupted). Relationships (all cascade 'all, delete-orphan'): jobs (order_by Job.id), review_rows (order_by idx), summaries (order_by idx). Invariants: status single-writer (job service); active_job property = the at-most-one queued/running job. listing() shape for landing page.

items.name: Job model (table `jobs`)
items.detail: models.py:100-137. Cols: id INT PK, document_id VARCHAR(36) FK->documents.id indexed, kind VARCHAR(16) (JOB_KINDS=segment,summarize), state VARCHAR(16) default 'queued' (JOB_STATES=queued,running,done,error,interrupted), stage VARCHAR(32) default 'starting', current INT, total INT, error TEXT, model VARCHAR(64) NOT NULL, prompt_version VARCHAR(16) NOT NULL, catalog_revision INT NULLABLE (nullable for pre-catalog jobs), created_at/started_at/finished_at DateTime. Relationship segment_rows cascade 'all, delete-orphan' order_by idx. Doubles as PROVENANCE (model + prompt_version + catalog_revision) for its rows. progress() dict for polling.

items.name: SegmentRow model (table `segment_rows`)
items.detail: models.py:140-160. RAW segmentation output, IMMUTABLE training INPUT (never edited by UI). Cols: id INT PK, job_id INT FK->jobs.id indexed, idx INT, start INT, end INT, category VARCHAR(8), title VARCHAR(512) default '-', date VARCHAR(16) default '-', injury_date VARCHAR(16) default '-', flag VARCHAR(4) default '-', suggest_merge BOOLEAN default 0. as_row() emits ROW_FIELDS+suggest_merge. Half of the training flywheel (input side, keyed by owning Job's model/prompt version).

items.name: ReviewRow model (table `review_rows`)
items.detail: models.py:163-191. Human-corrected working set = training LABEL and the summarize INPUT. Cols mirror SegmentRow (idx,start,end,category,title,date,injury_date,flag,suggest_merge) but keyed on document_id VARCHAR(36) FK->documents.id indexed (NOT job_id), plus include BOOLEAN default 1 (scopes which rows reach summarization). as_row() adds suggest_merge + include. Diff vs SegmentRow per document = the fine-tuning dataset.

items.name: Summary model (table `summaries`)
items.detail: models.py:194-247. Cols: id INT PK, document_id VARCHAR(36) FK->documents.id indexed, job_id INT FK->jobs.id NOT NULL, idx INT, title VARCHAR(512) NOT NULL + date VARCHAR(16) + text TEXT (RAW immutable model output), source_text TEXT nullable (exact extracted input the model saw = fine-tuning input half, captured at gen time, never shown), edited_title/edited_date/edited_text nullable (reviewer overrides that win in display/export), excluded BOOLEAN default 0 (dimmed in UI, omitted from Word export), manual_check BOOLEAN default 0, row_start/row_end INT + row_category VARCHAR(8) (snapshot of summarized row). effective_*/listing() methods. NOTE: job_id FK has NO relationship/cascade from Job (deleted only via Document cascade).

items.name: Category model (table `categories`)
items.detail: models.py:250-278. Editable taxonomy. Cols: id VARCHAR(8) PK (numeric string, IMMUTABLE), name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL, description TEXT default '', examples JSON default [] (db.JSON), active BOOLEAN default 1, auto_assign BOOLEAN default 1, updated_at DateTime onupdate. Two-set invariant: classifier uses active AND auto_assign; editor offers all active (incl id 6 which is selectable but never auto-assigned); active=False = soft-delete (hidden but id + historical rows preserved). listing() dict.

items.name: Prompt model (table `prompts`)
items.detail: models.py:281-295. Editable prompts. Cols: id INT PK, role VARCHAR(32) NOT NULL, category_id VARCHAR(8) NULLABLE, text TEXT NOT NULL, revision INT default 1, updated_at DateTime onupdate. UniqueConstraint(role, category_id) = uq_prompt_role_category. Phase 1 stores only role='summary' with category_id set; category_id NULL reserved for phase-2 global segmentation/verify prompts (added without migration). Id 11 intentionally has NO row -> falls back to general (100).

items.name: CatalogMeta model (table `catalog_meta`)
items.detail: models.py:298-308. Single-row (id always 1) monotonic revision counter. Cols: id INT PK, revision INT NOT NULL default 1. Bumped on any category/prompt edit; used to invalidate classifier in-memory caches AND to stamp Job.catalog_revision so outputs stay traceable.

items.name: AuditLog model (table `audit_log`)
items.detail: models.py:311-321. Who-did-what. Cols: id INT PK, user_id INT FK->user.id indexed, action VARCHAR(32), document_id VARCHAR(36) NULLABLE (id only, NOT a FK - references ids never filenames, so it survives document deletion), at DateTime default utcnow. Deliberately no cascade (retained after document delete).

items.name: SQLite PRAGMA connect event listener
items.detail: extensions.py:24-34. @event.listens_for(Engine,'connect') applies on every connection: journal_mode=WAL (persistent, DB-level), busy_timeout=5000 (per-connection), foreign_keys=ON (per-connection). Rationale: background job threads write progress while request threads read; without WAL the rollback-journal writer lock makes readers error instead of wait. Framework-agnostic SQLAlchemy event - logic ports directly, but must be re-registered on the FastAPI-owned Engine and on every worker process's engine.

items.name: db = SQLAlchemy() singleton + engine/session wiring
items.detail: extensions.py:21; bound to app in __init__.py:60-62 via db.init_app(app). This is flask_sqlalchemy's app-scoped session/engine management (db.session, db.Model, app-context-scoped sessions). Under FastAPI this becomes a plain SQLAlchemy Engine + sessionmaker + declarative Base with request-scoped sessions (dependency-injected) and separate engines in the Redis worker processes. DB URI from config.py:35-37 (sqlite:///instance/mrr.db default).

items.name: _create_schema boot migration (create_all + additive ALTER)
items.detail: __init__.py:108-136. On every boot: mkdir the sqlite dir, db.create_all() (cannot ALTER existing tables), then for each _ADDITIVE_COLUMNS table introspect PRAGMA table_info and ADD COLUMN any missing one, commit. Explicitly a stopgap that covers ADD COLUMN only; renames/drops/backfills are called out as the trigger to adopt Alembic. Runs create_all/DDL on startup - problematic when web + N externalized workers all boot concurrently against one SQLite file.

items.name: _ADDITIVE_COLUMNS declarative migration map
items.detail: __init__.py:94-105. Hand-maintained {table: [(col, ddl)]} of columns added after first ship: user.name VARCHAR(255), user.is_admin BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT 0, jobs.catalog_revision INTEGER, review_rows.include BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT 1, summaries.{edited_title VARCHAR(512), edited_date VARCHAR(16), edited_text TEXT, excluded BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT 0, source_text TEXT}. This is the full boot-migration history that Alembic must reproduce as its baseline so existing DBs are not re-migrated.

items.name: seed_catalog seed-if-empty mechanism
items.detail: seed_catalog.py:56-78. Runs every boot but populates only when Category.query.first() is None (never clobbers edited rows). Inserts constants_categories() (taxonomy ids w/ active=True,auto_assign=True + hardcoded id 6 SOAP notes active/not-auto_assign), one summary Prompt per category id that has a hardcoded prompt (id 11 skipped -> falls to 100), and CatalogMeta(id=1,revision=1). constants_categories()/constants_summary_prompt() (seed_catalog.py:26-53) also serve as runtime fallbacks and are framework-agnostic (port-as-is); the seed-on-boot trigger becomes an Alembic data migration or FastAPI startup task (rewrite).

items.name: DB-first catalog accessor + revision counter
items.detail: catalog.py (whole file). get_categories(active_only,auto_assign)/get_category_ids/get_category_options read Category rows (fallback to constants when unseeded); get_prompt(role,category_id) DB-first with summary->100 fallback then constants; catalog_version() reads CatalogMeta id=1 (catalog.py:63-66); bump_revision() increments+commits, creating the row if absent (catalog.py:69-80). Pure SQLAlchemy over db.session - moves to FastAPI on the injected Session unchanged apart from how the session is obtained.

portingNotes: Reuse the existing SQLite DB byte-compatibly: the new declarative models MUST reproduce the exact table/column names and types. The nine plain models copy verbatim (swap `db.Model` for a shared DeclarativeBase, `db.Column`->`Column`, `db.relationship`->`relationship`). Table names are pinned via __tablename__ (documents, jobs, segment_rows, review_rows, summaries, categories, prompts, catalog_meta, audit_log) and default names (`user`, `role`, `roles_users`) - keep all identical.

Role/User are the only true rewrites: they inherit Flask-Security fsqla_v3 mixins, so their column set is NOT visible in app code (only `name` + `is_admin` are). Before porting, introspect the LIVE mrr.db (PRAGMA table_info(user)/table_info(role)/table_info(roles_users)) and mirror every column exactly in plain SQLAlchemy models, or auth + DB-reuse breaks. Preserve `password` (hash), `fs_uniquifier`, `active`, `email` so existing logins survive; the new FastAPI auth layer re-implements verification against that same hash format.

Engine/session: create one SQLAlchemy Engine + sessionmaker on a shared DeclarativeBase; use request-scoped sessions in FastAPI (Depends) and independent engines/sessions in each Redis worker process. Do NOT share a Session across threads/async tasks. flask_sqlalchemy's implicit app-context session (db.session) has no FastAPI analog - every catalog.py/service call that used db.session must receive an explicit Session.

Re-register the SQLite PRAGMA listener (extensions.py:24-34) on EVERY engine (web + each worker). journal_mode=WAL is persistent once set, but busy_timeout and foreign_keys=ON are per-connection and must be applied on each new connection or FK cascades and writer-wait behavior silently differ. This is the load-bearing concurrency guarantee once the job queue is externalized to separate worker processes.

Boot migration -> Alembic: replace _create_schema's create_all + additive-ALTER with an Alembic baseline that encodes the current full schema INCLUDING every _ADDITIVE_COLUMNS entry, then `alembic stamp` existing production DBs to head so they are recognized as up-to-date and never re-migrated. New DBs run migrations from empty. Only ONE process should run migrations at startup (not every worker).

Seed -> idempotent startup/data-migration: keep the seed-if-empty semantics (only when categories empty; never clobber edited rows). constants_categories/constants_summary_prompt and the taxonomy.py/prompts.py constants stay as the seed source AND runtime fallback - port them unchanged. Preserve the exact quirks: id 6 (SOAP notes) active but auto_assign=False, id 11 has no prompt row and falls back to id 100.

ORM-level cascades (delete-orphan on Document->jobs/review_rows/summaries and Job->segment_rows) run in Python and port unchanged, but they only fire when objects are loaded through the ORM Session; bulk deletes bypass them. Keep foreign_keys=ON so DB-level integrity still holds for the FK-without-cascade edges (Summary.job_id, AuditLog.document_id is id-only not a FK).

Preserve the training-data design as an invariant, not an incidental detail: SegmentRow is immutable model output keyed to a Job (provenance = model+prompt_version+catalog_revision); ReviewRow is the mutable human label; Summary keeps raw text immutable with edited_* overrides and captures source_text at generation time. The FastAPI write paths must uphold the single-writer Document.status rule and the at-most-one-active-job invariant that today live in the job service.
risks: fsqla_v3 (Flask-Security) generates the user/role/roles_users schema implicitly; the exact columns/types are not in the repo. If the FastAPI port re-declares them from memory rather than introspecting the live DB, column drift (missing fs_uniquifier, wrong types, missing 2FA/unified-signin columns) will break DB reuse and/or auth silently.

No Alembic exists today. If the port keeps create_all() (or lets it run alongside Alembic) it can recreate/shadow tables or race; and without a baseline `stamp`, Alembic may try to re-create an already-populated schema. Getting the baseline+stamp wrong risks data loss on the seeded/production DB.

Externalizing the queue multiplies SQLite writers: create_all/ALTER on startup from multiple processes, and concurrent writes from N workers + web, can hit `database is locked` despite busy_timeout=5000. SQLite is a single-writer store; the WAL/busy_timeout tuning was sized for 2 in-process threads (PIPELINE_WORKERS=2), not separate processes/hosts.

SQLite WAL requires all readers/writers on the same local filesystem; it does not work reliably over network shares. If FastAPI and the Redis workers run on different hosts pointing at a shared-mount mrr.db, WAL breaks. Keeping SQLite likely forces web+workers co-located on one box, or a move off SQLite.

Summary.job_id is a FK to jobs.id with no relationship/cascade from Job; today summaries are only deleted via the Document cascade. A FastAPI path that deletes a Job directly (e.g. re-run cleanup) could orphan or FK-violate summaries - the current safety is implicit, not enforced by the model.

Datetime tz handling: _utcnow() returns tz-aware UTC datetimes but SQLite stores them as naive strings; SQLAlchemy reads them back naive. A stricter FastAPI/Pydantic layer that assumes tz-aware values could misbehave or double-convert. Behavior must be matched, not 'fixed', to keep existing rows consistent.

Category.examples uses db.JSON (stored as TEXT in SQLite). The port must use the same JSON type/serialization so existing rows deserialize identically; a switch to a native JSON/JSONB or different encoder would corrupt reads of already-stored examples.
openQuestions: Which Flask-Security version / fsqla_v3 schema is in the live DB? Need PRAGMA table_info(user), table_info(role), table_info(roles_users) from a real mrr.db to author byte-compatible FastAPI models (columns like fs_uniquifier, fs_token_uniquifier, tf_*, us_*, mf_recovery_codes vary by version).

Should the boot migration/seed move to Alembic migrations or a FastAPI lifespan startup hook, and which single process owns DDL/seed once web and the Redis workers are separate processes (to avoid concurrent create_all/ALTER on one SQLite file)?

Will the externalized worker processes run on the same host/filesystem as the web app? This decides whether SQLite WAL remains viable or the 'keep the same DB' constraint forces co-location (or an eventual move to Postgres/SQL Server the URI comment anticipates).

What is the target session model under FastAPI - request-scoped Session via Depends for the web tier and a separate per-task/per-worker Session in the queue tier - and how are the shared framework-agnostic services (catalog.py etc.) refactored to accept an explicit Session instead of the implicit flask_sqlalchemy db.session?

How are existing password hashes / fs_uniquifier values carried into the re-implemented auth layer so current users can log in without a reset (the auth is a rewrite but the credential columns must be preserved)?

Confirm all data-model paths here are the multi-user (accounts) design - none appear to depend on state.py single-user globals, so nothing in this track is a 'drop'; verify no legacy single-user table/column is still present in shipped DBs.

result.summary: The app uses Flask-Security-Too 5.8.1 (flask-security[common,fsqla]) for the entire auth surface: open self-registration, session-cookie login/logout, CSRF, and the user datastore, layered on Flask-Login + Flask-WTF. Authorization is enforced app-wide by ONE before_request deny-by-default gate in mrr_ai/security.py (everything outside PUBLIC_ENDPOINTS requires a session; /admin and /api/admin additionally require the is_admin boolean), NOT per-route decorators - so the FastAPI port must reproduce a global gate, not scatter guards. The session is a Flask signed cookie (SECRET_KEY) holding the user's fs_uniquifier via Flask-Login; CSRF uses the Angular-style double-submit pattern (readable XSRF-TOKEN cookie out, X-XSRF-Token header back in) wired by CSRFProtect BEFORE Security. Passwords use Flask-Security's default hash (argon2 via the [common] extra) salted by SECURITY_PASSWORD_SALT, with a custom MrrPasswordUtil enforcing 8+ chars plus a digit and a symbol on new passwords only (never at login). Per-user isolation is an IDOR-safe owner-check (filter_by id AND current_user.id -> 404, never 403) repeated across documents_api.py. The single biggest port decision is session-cookie vs JWT: Next.js is a separate origin/build and Flask-Security has no drop-in FastAPI equivalent, so login, logout, registration, the CSRF scheme, current_user, and the two gates must all be re-implemented while KEEPING the existing SQLite User/Role schema (fsqla mixins + name + is_admin) and, ideally, the existing argon2 hashes so no user must reset. All of this is classic web auth (not the state.py single-user path), so nothing here is "drop".

files.role: Auth wiring: CSRFProtect + Flask-Security init, MrrRegisterForm (adds required name), MrrPasswordUtil (custom password rules), the before_request deny-by-default gate, PUBLIC_ENDPOINTS, and the /admin + /api/admin is_admin gate.
App factory: all SECURITY_*/session-cookie/CSRF config in create_app (lines 29-56); additive user-table column migration for name + is_admin (lines 94-105).
Secrets + fail-fast validate_env: SECRET_KEY and SECURITY_PASSWORD_SALT are REQUIRED (lines 9, 30-31, 104-112); SQLALCHEMY_DATABASE_URI (SQLite kept).
User/Role on fsqla_v3 mixins (lines 16-35); custom columns name (nullable) + is_admin (bool). Document/AuditLog carry user_id FKs used by owner-checks.
flask admin CLI (AppGroup): grant/revoke/list admin accounts - the only way to bootstrap the first admin (self-registration never sets is_admin).
The owner-check IDOR guard _own() (lines 36-38) and current_user usage for per-user upload dirs, listings, and dedupe.
/api/admin routes (all pre-gated by is_admin); /api/admin/whoami returns email + is_admin for the UI (lines 29-32).
/admin page render relies on the app gate for authorization (lines 92-96); comment documents the no-re-check assumption.
audit() reads current_user.id when no user_id passed (lines 11-17) - a hidden dependency on the auth context.
Custom Flask-Security login template (form posts to url_for_security('login'), hidden CSRF via hidden_tag(), remember-me).
Custom registration template (name/email/password/confirm; loads register.js for live checklist).
Auth-page shell (Evaluators design system) that login/register extend.
Client-side password checklist (8+/number/symbol) + confirm-match; mirrors MrrPasswordUtil, gates submit (server still enforces).
Front-end fetch wrapper (lines 85-107): reads XSRF-TOKEN cookie, sends X-XSRF-Token on unsafe methods, redirects to /login on 401.
Same CSRF cookie->header + 401-redirect pattern for /api/admin (lines 7-25).
Auth test harness: login() helper POSTs /login (lines 91-95); authenticated client vs anon_client fixtures; test app disables CSRF and forces plaintext hash (lines 52-72, 98-110).

items.name: Open self-registration (SECURITY_REGISTERABLE, not confirmable, no email)
items.detail: __init__.py:41-43 SECURITY_REGISTERABLE=True, SECURITY_CONFIRMABLE=False, SECURITY_SEND_REGISTER_EMAIL=False (no SMTP on box). Anyone can create an ordinary (non-admin) account; account is active immediately, no email verification. SECURITY_EMAIL_VALIDATOR_ARGS check_deliverability=False (__init__.py:47) disables live DNS/MX so reserved/test domains pass. Endpoint 'security.register' is public (security.py:25).

items.name: Required display name at registration (MrrRegisterForm)
items.detail: security.py:37-43 subclasses RegisterFormV2 adding name = StringField(validators=[DataRequired, Length(max=255)]). User.name column (models.py:31) matches so Flask-Security's to_dict(only_user=True) passes it straight to create_user. Must be collected + validated on the port's register flow.

items.name: Custom password rules (MrrPasswordUtil)
items.detail: security.py:46-61 extends PasswordUtil.validate: keeps the stock 8+ length check, then requires a digit (\d) and a symbol ([^A-Za-z0-9]). Applies to NEW passwords only (register/change/reset), never at login. Mirrored client-side in register.js:13-17 (advisory only). The two regex checks are framework-agnostic Python and can move as a plain validator function.

items.name: Password hashing scheme + salt (argon2 default)
items.detail: No SECURITY_PASSWORD_HASH set, so Flask-Security-Too 5.8 default (argon2, via the [common] extra in pyproject.toml:25) is used; SECURITY_PASSWORD_SALT (config.py:31, REQUIRED) is the HMAC salt Flask-Security prepends. Stored as a passlib-style hash string in User.password. To avoid forcing every user to reset, the FastAPI port must verify existing hashes with the same argon2 params + salt handling (passlib/argon2-cffi). CONFIDENCE MEDIUM on exact default - confirm against the live DB hash prefix.

items.name: Login endpoint + POST-login view (content-negotiated)
items.detail: POST/GET /login provided by Flask-Security (endpoint 'security.login', public - security.py:25). __init__.py:48 SECURITY_POST_LOGIN_VIEW='/'. Content negotiation: form POST -> 302 to '/'; Accept: application/json -> JSON body incl. a fresh CSRF token. Front-end relies on the JSON/401 behavior (review.js:103).

items.name: Logout endpoint + POST-logout view
items.detail: GET /logout (Flask-Security). __init__.py:50 SECURITY_POST_LOGOUT_VIEW='/login'. Linked from every app template's nav (home/review/bundle/admin.html). Clears the session; Flask-Security also rotates fs_uniquifier semantics on relevant transitions. NOTE /logout is NOT in PUBLIC_ENDPOINTS but is reachable because the caller is authenticated.

items.name: Session cookie configuration
items.detail: __init__.py:36-37 SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY=True, SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE='Lax'. SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE intentionally DEFERRED (comment lines 34-35: must be added the day HTTPS is served; setting now breaks plain-HTTP LAN). Cookie is a Flask itsdangerous-signed session (SECRET_KEY, config.py:30) storing the user identity (fs_uniquifier via Flask-Login get_id). KEY DECISION for the port: keep an HttpOnly session cookie vs move to JWT.

items.name: CSRF: XSRF-TOKEN cookie out, X-XSRF-Token header in
items.detail: security.py:69 CSRFProtect(app) initialized BEFORE Security (official pattern). __init__.py:52 SECURITY_CSRF_COOKIE_NAME='XSRF-TOKEN' (readable, NOT HttpOnly - JS reads it via document.cookie). __init__.py:55 WTF_CSRF_TIME_LIMIT=None (token lives as long as the session; avoids mid-review expiry eating edits). Flask-Security accepts the token echoed in the X-XSRF-Token header (front-end: review.js:95, admin.js:14, bundle-page.js, doc-table.js, review.js:883). Token is session-bound + SECRET_KEY-signed (not pure stateless double-submit).

items.name: Deny-by-default before_request gate + PUBLIC_ENDPOINTS
items.detail: security.py:82-93 @app.before_request: endpoint None -> return None (let Flask 404, reveal nothing); endpoint in PUBLIC_ENDPOINTS {security.login, security.register, static} (security.py:25) -> allow; else if not current_user.is_authenticated -> app.login_manager.unauthorized() (content-negotiates: browser 302 /login, JSON 401). This app-level gate is the core invariant: new routes are protected by default. Port must replicate as global middleware, not per-route deps.

items.name: Admin gate on /admin + /api/admin (is_admin)
items.detail: security.py:30-34 ADMIN_PATH_PREFIXES=('/admin','/api/admin') with prefix/exact matching; security.py:91-92 after auth, if _is_admin_path(request.path) and not current_user.is_admin -> abort(403). is_admin is a single boolean (models.py:35), NOT full RBAC. Handlers in admin_api.py and pages.py deliberately do NOT re-check (they rely on this gate).

items.name: current_user proxy access
items.detail: flask_security.current_user (Flask-Login LocalProxy) read in security.py:88/91, admin_api.py:32, documents_api.py (id in _own, upload dir, dedupe), audit.py:16, and templates (current_user.is_admin nav gate in home/review/bundle.html). Port needs a request-scoped current-user accessor (FastAPI dependency) available to routes, services, and any templating.

items.name: Owner-check / IDOR guard (_own)
items.detail: documents_api.py:36-38 _own() = Document.query.filter_by(id=document_id, user_id=current_user.id).first(); miss -> 404 (_not_found, line 41-42), NEVER 403, so a non-owner can't confirm existence. Applied on every document route (get/delete/pdf/status/rows/segment/summarize/summaries/export/bundle). The predicate is framework-agnostic; only the current_user.id source changes.

items.name: SQLAlchemyUserDatastore (user store)
items.detail: security.py:74 SQLAlchemyUserDatastore(db, User, Role); create_user/find_user etc. Used at registration and in tests (conftest.py:83-87 datastore.create_user). Port needs an equivalent user-repository over the SAME tables (create/find/activate, fs_uniquifier generation).

items.name: User/Role model on fsqla_v3 mixins (session identity)
items.detail: models.py:16-35: Role(FsRoleMixin), User(FsUserMixin) + name (nullable) + is_admin (bool, default False). fsqla_v3 supplies email, password, active, fs_uniquifier, confirmed_at, last_login_*/current_login_* + tf_/us_ MFA columns. fs_uniquifier is the value stored in the session cookie (Flask-Login get_id) and rotating it invalidates sessions. DB/schema is KEPT, so these columns must survive; the port re-uses the table as-is.

items.name: flask admin CLI (grant/revoke/list)
items.detail: cli.py:17-63 AppGroup 'admin' with grant/revoke/list; the ONLY bootstrap for the first admin (registration never sets is_admin). Runs inside an app context. Port needs an equivalent CLI/management script hitting the same User.is_admin column.

items.name: whoami confirmation endpoint
items.detail: admin_api.py:29-32 GET /api/admin/whoami -> {email, is_admin}. Used by the admin UI + tests to confirm admin reachability. Trivial to reproduce once current_user + the admin gate exist.

items.name: Audit trail bound to current_user
items.detail: audit.py:11-17 audit(action, document_id=None, user_id=None): if user_id is None it imports flask_security.current_user and uses current_user.id. Every mutating document/admin route calls audit(...). Rows store user_id + action + document_id only (never filenames - PHI). Port must supply current_user.id here from the new auth context.

items.name: Server-rendered auth UI (login/register/base templates)
items.detail: templates/security/{login_user,register_user,base}.html override Flask-Security defaults with the Evaluators design system. login posts email/password/remember; register posts name/email/password/password_confirm and loads register.js. In Next.js these become client-rendered pages calling the FastAPI auth endpoints.

items.name: Remember-me cookie
items.detail: login_user.html:34 exposes login_user_form.remember -> Flask-Login 'remember me' issues a separate long-lived signed remember_token cookie that re-establishes the session. Port must decide whether to reproduce persistent login (refresh token / long-lived cookie) or drop it.

items.name: Front-end CSRF + 401 fetch convention
items.detail: review.js:85-107 / admin.js:7-25 / bundle-page.js / doc-table.js: read XSRF-TOKEN cookie via document.cookie, attach X-XSRF-Token on non-GET, and on 401 do window.location='/login'. This client contract (readable CSRF cookie + header + 401->login) must be reproduced by the Next.js data layer, or replaced by the port's chosen auth transport.

items.name: Fail-fast secret validation
items.detail: config.py:9,104-112 REQUIRED_ENV includes SECRET_KEY + SECURITY_PASSWORD_SALT; validate_env() raises RuntimeError if missing; called at extensions.py:19 import time. Framework-agnostic; the FastAPI app should keep the same startup guard (and the same SECRET_KEY/SALT values if it wants to read existing sessions/hashes).

items.name: Test auth harness (login helper + fixtures)
items.detail: conftest.py: login() posts real /login (91-95); client fixture is authenticated, anon_client tests the gate (98-110); test app sets WTF_CSRF_ENABLED=False + SECURITY_PASSWORD_HASH='plaintext' (52-72). The port needs equivalent auth test fixtures (a way to mint an authenticated client and to run the deny-by-default gate unauthenticated).

items.name: Additive user-table migration (name, is_admin)
items.detail: __init__.py:94-105 _ADDITIVE_COLUMNS adds user.name VARCHAR(255) and user.is_admin BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT 0 via boot-time ALTER TABLE (SQLite stopgap, not Alembic). Since the DB/schema is KEPT, the columns already exist; the port only needs to read them, not re-run this.

portingNotes: KEY DECISION - session cookie vs JWT: current design is an HttpOnly, SECRET_KEY-signed server session cookie (SameSite=Lax, Secure deferred) holding fs_uniquifier. Same-origin deploy (Next.js and FastAPI behind one host/reverse-proxy) lets you keep an HttpOnly session cookie almost 1:1 and preserve SameSite=Lax + the readable-CSRF-cookie pattern. Cross-origin (Next.js on a different domain than the API) forces SameSite=None; Secure and CORS with credentials, and makes CSRF/cookie handling harder - which is what pushes teams toward Bearer JWT in an Authorization header (no CSRF cookie needed). Recommend documenting deploy topology first, because it dictates the whole auth transport.

Candidate FastAPI auth libraries: (1) FastAPI-Users - closest to Flask-Security (registration, login/logout, password hashing via argon2/bcrypt through passlib/pwdlib, SQLAlchemy user model, cookie OR JWT transports, is_superuser flag maps onto is_admin); it can target the existing SQLite User table but the fsqla column names (fs_uniquifier, active, confirmed_at, tf_/us_) won't line up with its defaults without a custom model + user-manager. (2) Authlib / fastapi-login / starlette SessionMiddleware for a thinner hand-rolled cookie session. (3) A minimal custom layer (itsdangerous-signed cookie + passlib argon2 verify) that reuses SECRET_KEY/SALT so existing sessions AND existing password hashes remain valid.

Password-hash continuity: to avoid forcing every user to reset, the port MUST verify Flask-Security's stored hashes. Inspect a real User.password prefix in the live DB to confirm the scheme (expected argon2, e.g. $argon2id$...), then configure passlib/pwdlib with the same scheme + SECURITY_PASSWORD_SALT handling. Flask-Security HMACs the password with the salt before hashing in some configs - replicate exactly or verification fails.

Deny-by-default gate -> FastAPI middleware, NOT per-route Depends: the security guarantee is that a newly added route is protected unless explicitly whitelisted. Reproduce with a global middleware (or a router-level dependency applied to the whole app) that allows an allow-list (login, register, static, health) and 401s everything else; layer the is_admin path-prefix check for /admin and /api/admin on top. Preserve the content-negotiation nuance: HTML/browser requests 302 to the login page, application/json requests get 401 JSON (the front-end depends on 401->redirect).

current_user in services: audit.py and the owner-checks pull current_user out of a global proxy. FastAPI has no thread-local request proxy by default - inject the current user via a dependency and PASS it down to services (audit(action, doc_id, user)), or use a contextvar-backed request context. This is a mechanical but pervasive change touching audit.py and every documents_api owner-check.

CSRF in the port: if you keep cookie-based sessions, reproduce the readable XSRF-TOKEN cookie + X-XSRF-Token header double-submit (starlette-csrf or a small dependency); the Next.js fetch layer already expects to read a cookie and echo a header (review.js/admin.js), so an axios/fetch interceptor replicates the client side. If you move to Authorization: Bearer JWT, CSRF protection is unnecessary and the XSRF cookie/header can be dropped entirely - but then Next.js must store/attach the token (memory + refresh, or an HttpOnly cookie read server-side in Next route handlers).

Next.js session holding: with HttpOnly cookies the browser holds the session automatically and Next.js Server Components/route handlers can forward the cookie to FastAPI; the SPA never sees the token (best for PHI). With JWT you either keep it in memory (lost on reload, needs refresh) or in an HttpOnly cookie set by a Next.js route handler acting as a BFF. Given HIPAA, prefer HttpOnly cookies over localStorage tokens.

Keep the same DB/schema: User/Role are fsqla_v3 tables; do not regenerate them. The port reuses email/password/active/fs_uniquifier/name/is_admin as-is. fs_uniquifier should remain the session identity so existing sessions and the 'rotate to force logout' capability survive; if the port stops using it, document that all existing sessions are invalidated on cutover.

Registration open + no email confirmation is a current product state tied to 'no SMTP on this box' - carry the open/confirmable flags as explicit config in the port so flipping to confirmable later is a config change, and mirror check_deliverability=False so test/reserved domains still register.

Admin bootstrap: the flask admin CLI has no HTTP surface; reproduce it as a FastAPI management command / script (e.g. python -m app.admin grant email) that flips User.is_admin on the same table, since self-registration never grants admin.
risks: PHI/session security: this app processes medical records, so a weakened auth port is a HIPAA exposure. Regressions to watch on cutover: forgetting SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE once HTTPS is live (currently deferred), storing JWTs in localStorage (XSS-exfiltratable) instead of HttpOnly cookies, or a cross-origin config that loosens SameSite=Lax to None without tight CORS.

Losing the deny-by-default invariant: if the port switches to per-route dependencies instead of a global gate, a future route can ship unauthenticated silently. The current before_request design specifically prevents this - the port must keep an equivalent global default-deny or it is a strictly weaker security posture.

IDOR regressions: the owner-check returns 404 (not 403) on foreign ids so existence isn't leaked. A naive port that 403s, or that resolves the document before checking ownership, reintroduces an IDOR/enumeration channel across patient records.

Password-hash incompatibility: if the FastAPI hasher can't verify Flask-Security's stored argon2 hashes (wrong scheme/params/salt handling), either every user is locked out or (worse) a fallback silently accepts weaker credentials. Must be verified against a real hash before cutover.

CSRF gap during transition: the XSRF-TOKEN cookie is intentionally non-HttpOnly (JS reads it). If the port keeps cookie sessions but drops or misconfigures the CSRF header check, state-changing endpoints become CSRF-vulnerable; if it keeps the readable cookie but no longer validates it, it's security theater.

fs_uniquifier / session-format change invalidates all live sessions and remember-me cookies at cutover - acceptable but must be communicated; also the remember-me long-lived cookie is a standing credential that needs the same Secure/HttpOnly treatment.

current_user removal from services: audit logging and owner-checks depend on an ambient current user; if a service path is ported without threading the user through, audit rows could be written with a wrong/None user_id or owner-checks could be bypassed.
openQuestions: Deploy topology: will Next.js and FastAPI be served same-origin (one host / reverse proxy) or cross-origin (separate domains)? This determines cookie-session vs JWT and the entire CSRF/CORS design.

Confirm the actual stored password hash scheme by inspecting a real User.password value in the live SQLite DB (expected argon2 via [common], but SECURITY_PASSWORD_HASH is unset so it must be verified) - and confirm whether Flask-Security HMACs with SECURITY_PASSWORD_SALT before hashing, so the port can reproduce verification exactly.

Is preserving existing users' passwords (no forced reset) a hard requirement? If yes, the port is constrained to an argon2-compatible verifier + the same salt; if a reset is acceptable, the hasher choice is free.
Should remember-me / persistent login be preserved, or is a session-only login acceptable in the port?

Should the port keep fs_uniquifier as the session identity (preserving the 'rotate to force logout' capability and existing sessions), or is a clean session-format cutover (invalidating all current sessions) acceptable?

Registration is currently fully open and non-confirmable because there's no SMTP - does the target deployment add mail (enabling email confirmation), or should the port keep open/non-confirmable registration?
Is the single is_admin boolean sufficient for the target, or does the re-platform intend to introduce real role-based access (which would change the Role table usage and the admin gate)?
Does the FastAPI side need Jinja-equivalent server rendering of any auth page, or are login/register fully owned by Next.js (API-only auth endpoints on FastAPI)?

result.summary: The async pipeline has TWO runners. The live multi-user one is services/job_queue.py: a DB-backed queue where jobs persist as Job rows and execute on an in-process ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=PIPELINE_WORKERS, default 2), created in init_app and stored on app.extensions["job_queue"]. submit(document_id, kind, target, ...) is the only entry: under a module-level threading.Lock (_submit_lock) it runs a check-then-insert (active_job() must be None), sets Document.status, commits the Job, then submits _run to the pool. _run wraps the target in app.app_context() (each pool thread gets its own scoped session), flips Job.state running->done/error, and drives the Document.status state machine (job_queue is the SINGLE writer of status after upload). Progress flows through a report(stage,current,total) callback that writes Job.stage/current/total to the DB, throttled to PROGRESS_MIN_INTERVAL=1.0s (except on stage change) because SQLite has one writer. sweep_orphans() runs at boot, marking any queued/running Job (and its Document) "interrupted" so a crash cannot leave phantom running rows. Provenance (model, prompt_version, catalog_revision) is stamped onto the Job at submit. The whole design assumes ONE process (serve.py runs waitress single-process; the classic runner services/jobs.py + state.py globals reinforce it). The classifier keeps a per-process in-memory catalog cache + sentence-transformers embedding matrix, invalidated by polling catalog_version() from the DB. Disposition: REDESIGN for Redis + external worker processes.

files.role: DB-backed job queue: ThreadPoolExecutor pool, submit()+_submit_lock, _run() app-context worker, state machine, progress throttle, sweep_orphans() - the primary REDESIGN target

Job targets _segment_target (204-234) + _summarize_target (255-299), submit() call sites segment_start/summarize_start, status polling endpoint (179-185); also synchronous non-queued LLM paths resummarize (369-425) + bundle_summarize (549-595)
Job (provenance + progress columns, 100-137), SegmentRow (immutable raw output, 140-160), Document.status state machine + active_job property + single-writer rule (42-97)
run_segmentation engine: Flask-free, inner ThreadPoolExecutor(CLASSIFY_WORKERS) for per-row categorization, progress callback contract - port-as-is

In-process catalog cache + embedding matrix as module globals (88-215), _catalog_lock/_embed_lock, revision-poll invalidation, lazy torch/sentence-transformers load - cross-process cache-coherence concern
PIPELINE_WORKERS(41), CLASSIFY_WORKERS(73), Vertex retry knobs (61-63), BUNDLE_SUMMARIZE_CAP(86) - concurrency + quota tuning
SQLite WAL + busy_timeout=5000 + foreign_keys pragmas (24-34) enabling concurrent reader/writer - must survive multi-process access
App-factory wiring: reset_catalog_cache() (80-82) then job_queue.init_app() (84-86) after schema exists
Production entry point documenting the HARD single-process constraint (waitress one process, threads=PIPELINE_WORKERS+6)
Classic single-slot in-memory runner (module globals _lock/_job) - DROP
Classic single-user mutable globals (pdf_filepath, all_data, num_pages...) - DROP; its existence is a reason for the single-process constraint
Classic segment/summarize endpoints driving services/jobs.py off state.py globals (53-119) - DROP
Vertex 429/5xx full-jitter backoff wrapper - port-as-is, still gates shared-quota storms
catalog_version() (63) + bump_revision() (69): provenance-stamp source AND classifier cache-invalidation trigger

items.name: ThreadPoolExecutor pool + init_app
items.detail: job_queue.init_app(app) at __init__.py:84-86 creates ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=PIPELINE_WORKERS, thread_name_prefix='pipeline') stored on app.extensions['job_queue'] (job_queue.py:44-50); PIPELINE_WORKERS default 2 (config.py:41) kept low because all jobs share one Vertex quota pool. This IS the executor being externalized to Redis + separate worker processes.

items.name: submit() + _submit_lock single-active-job invariant
items.detail: job_queue.py:37,82-111: _submit_lock (threading.Lock) serializes a check-then-insert - active_job(document_id) must be None (job_queue.py:73-79 queries Job.state in queued/running) before inserting the Job and pool.submit(_run). This in-process lock is the ONLY guard against two active jobs per document; it does not survive a move to multiple worker/web processes.

items.name: _run() worker: app context + scoped session + state machine
items.detail: job_queue.py:114-156: each pool thread runs target under app.app_context() with its own thread-scoped db.session; sets Job.state running->done/error, Job.started_at/finished_at, and on exception rollback()+Job.error+Document.status='error'. Drives _STATUS_ON_SUBMIT/_STATUS_ON_DONE transitions (job_queue.py:28-29). Target is passed as a live Python closure - not transferable across a process boundary.

items.name: progress report() + PROGRESS_MIN_INTERVAL throttle
items.detail: job_queue.py:33,125-136: report(stage,current,total) writes Job.stage/current/total to the DB; same-stage ticks rate-limited to 1.0s (stage changes always write) because SQLite has one writer and unthrottled progress contends with the job's own inserts. Engine calls it around every model interaction (segment_engine.py:141-165).

items.name: sweep_orphans() boot reconciliation
items.detail: job_queue.py:53-70, called from init_app under app_context: marks every Job with state in (queued,running) as 'interrupted' + its Document status 'interrupted', so a crash/restart cannot leave phantom running rows the UI polls forever or that block submit(). Assumes all running rows belong to the just-died single process.

items.name: Document.status state machine + single-writer rule
items.detail: models.py:42-50 DOCUMENT_STATUSES (uploaded,segmenting,reviewing,summarizing,done,error,interrupted); job_queue is the sole writer after 'uploaded'/delete (models.py:9-11, job_queue.py:9-11). status column models.py:67; Document.active_job property models.py:81-84. Schema stays (same SQLite DB) but the transition-driver code moves into workers.

items.name: Job model (progress + provenance record)
items.detail: models.py:100-137: id, document_id, kind, state, stage/current/total, error, model, prompt_version, catalog_revision (nullable), created/started/finished_at; progress() shape at 129-137. Doubles as the provenance record for its SegmentRows/Summaries. Same table reused unchanged against the kept SQLite schema.

items.name: SegmentRow model (immutable raw output)
items.detail: models.py:140-160: job_id FK, idx, start/end/category/title/date/injury_date/flag, suggest_merge; the immutable training INPUT half (ReviewRow is the label). Written by _segment_target keyed to the owning Job.id. Schema kept as-is.

items.name: _segment_target job body
items.detail: documents_api.py:204-234: closure target(report) runs run_segmentation(pdf_path,total_pages,progress=report), re-fetches job via job_queue.active_job(document_id) for job.id, deletes ReviewRows, inserts paired SegmentRow(job_id=...) + ReviewRow(document_id=...), commits. Persistence logic is portable but the closure-over-pdf_path + active_job re-fetch pattern must become a worker task keyed by document_id.

items.name: _summarize_target job body
items.detail: documents_api.py:255-299: closure target(report) loads ReviewRows where include=True, per-row catalog.get_prompt('summary',category) + summarize_engine.summarize_row(...), builds Summary rows (with job_id + source_text provenance), then replaces old summaries only after the new set is complete (failed run keeps prior results). Same closure/active_job pattern as segment; becomes a worker task.

items.name: run_segmentation engine + inner CLASSIFY_WORKERS pool
items.detail: segment_engine.py:134-169: Flask-free; sliding-window Vertex calls sequentially, then a NESTED ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=CLASSIFY_WORKERS=4, config.py:73) for per-row _categorize, then optional verify_and_merge. Takes a progress callback and raises loudly on worker failure. Moves into worker processes unchanged (pure Python + genai_client).

items.name: Classifier in-process catalog cache + embedding matrix
items.detail: classification.py:88-215: module-global _catalog_categories/_catalog_text_cache/_category_ids/_category_matrix guarded by _catalog_lock; sentence-transformers all-MiniLM-L6-v2 loaded lazily behind _embed_lock (encode serialized, not thread-safe). _refresh_locked() rebuilds when catalog_version() (DB) changes. reset_catalog_cache() called at app creation (__init__.py:80-82). Per-process state: N worker processes = N model+matrix copies; coherence relies on each process polling the DB revision.

items.name: Provenance stamping at submit
items.detail: documents_api.py:242-249,320-327: submit() records model=config.GENAI_MODEL/SUMMARY_MODEL, prompt_version=PROMPT_VERSION ('2', gemini.py:10), catalog_revision=catalog.catalog_version() onto the Job. source_text captured per Summary in the engine (summarize_engine.py:83). Must be passed in the enqueue payload so the worker records the revision current at submit time.

items.name: Status polling endpoint
items.detail: GET /api/documents/<id>/status documents_api.py:179-185: ownership-scoped, returns {status: Document.status, job: latest Job.progress()}. Also list_documents/get_document embed active_job progress (documents_api.py:96,118-134). Read-only DB query; re-implemented as a FastAPI route (auth+ownership rewrite).

items.name: segment_start / summarize_start submit endpoints
items.detail: POST /api/documents/<id>/segment/start (237-252) + /summarize/start (302-330): resolve owned doc, optionally flush editor rows (_store_rows), call job_queue.submit(), 409 if job is None (already active). summarize also gates on >=1 include row. Endpoints move to FastAPI and call the new enqueue API; the 409 semantics depend on the redesigned invariant.

items.name: Job-conflict 409 guards on mutation routes
items.detail: documents_api.py:153-154 (delete), 193-196 (put_rows), 311-312 (summarize), 351-352 (put_summary), 381-383 (resummarize): every mutating route refuses (409) while document.active_job is not None, because a finishing segment job would overwrite rows / a summarize job is reading them. This concurrency-guard behavior must be preserved against the externalized queue's notion of 'active'.

items.name: Synchronous non-queued LLM paths
items.detail: resummarize (documents_api.py:369-425: one OCR + two model calls, inline) and bundle_summarize (549-595: bounded by BUNDLE_SUMMARIZE_CAP=40, inline). These run on the request thread OUTSIDE the queue. Under FastAPI (async) blocking Vertex calls must move to a threadpool/worker; candidates to fold onto the same queue so they honor the active-job invariant + shared quota.

items.name: SQLite WAL + busy_timeout pragmas
items.detail: extensions.py:24-34: PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL, busy_timeout=5000, foreign_keys=ON on every connection, so background job threads writing progress and request threads reading do not error. Same DB is kept; pragmas must apply to every worker + web process connection, and busy_timeout is the only backpressure once writers multiply.

items.name: Vertex retry/backoff wrapper
items.detail: genai_retry.py: generate_with_retry() full-jitter exponential backoff over GENAI_MAX_RETRIES=6, retries transient 429/5xx/transport disconnects, re-raises PerDay/free_tier quota. Shared by segment + classify + summarize + verify. Framework-agnostic; moves to workers unchanged and is the main defense against 429 storms as concurrency grows.

items.name: catalog_version() / bump_revision() + reset_catalog_cache wiring
items.detail: catalog.py:63-80 CatalogMeta single-row monotonic revision; bump_revision() on any category/prompt edit. Dual role: provenance stamp (Job.catalog_revision) AND classifier cache-invalidation trigger (classification._refresh_locked polls it). reset_catalog_cache() wired at app creation (__init__.py:80-82). Value/table port unchanged; the cross-process cache-reset wiring is the redesign piece.

items.name: Single-process hard constraint
items.detail: serve.py:1-29 documents that ONE process is mandatory (in-process pool + state.py globals); threads=PIPELINE_WORKERS+6. Externalizing the queue is precisely what removes this constraint - web (FastAPI/uvicorn) and worker processes scale independently. The constraint itself does not port.

items.name: Classic single-slot runner (services/jobs.py)
items.detail: jobs.py:1-72: in-memory _lock/_job single-slot runner (start/status/clear) on a daemon Thread, honest only because the classic UI keeps one case in state.py globals. Used solely by review_api.py (10,62,72,109,116). CLASSIC single-user path - not worth porting.

items.name: Classic state.py globals + review_api endpoints
items.detail: state.py:13-24 module globals (pdf_filepath, all_data, num_pages, main_filename...) + review_api.py:45-119 (/api/pdf,/api/segment/*,/api/summarize/* off those globals). Single-user, single-process legacy flow superseded by the document-scoped DB API. Drop entirely on the multi-user Next.js+FastAPI target.

portingNotes: Executor -> broker: replace ThreadPoolExecutor + pool.submit(_run, app, job_id, target) with an RQ (Redis-only, simplest) or Celery task enqueue. The current target is a live closure over pdf_path/model and cannot cross a process boundary; redesign each job as a registered worker function taking a plain payload (document_id, kind, model, prompt_version, catalog_revision) that resolves Document/stored_path/page_count from the DB inside the worker.

Preserve one-job-per-document across processes: the in-process _submit_lock no longer works once web + workers are separate OS processes. Enforce the invariant in the DB itself - e.g. a partial UNIQUE index on jobs(document_id) WHERE state IN ('queued','running'), or wrap the check-then-insert in a transaction and let the constraint violation map to the existing 409. A Redis SETNX per-document lock is an alternative but the DB constraint is the durable source of truth and matches the 'status/progress via DB' requirement.

Status + progress stay in the DB: the FastAPI status route reads Document.status + latest Job.progress() with the identical query, so the polling contract is unchanged for the Next.js client. Keep writing Job.stage/current/total from the worker and keep PROGRESS_MIN_INTERVAL throttling - SQLite single-writer contention persists (arguably worsens) with multiple worker processes, so throttling is still load-bearing, not cosmetic.

State machine ownership: job_queue is currently the SOLE writer of Document.status after upload. In the target, the worker task must remain the only status writer (uploaded->segmenting/summarizing at enqueue is a web-side write; running->reviewing/done/error is worker-side). Keep the _STATUS_ON_SUBMIT/_STATUS_ON_DONE mapping and the on-exception rollback+error path intact so the single-writer guarantee survives.

Orphan recovery must become registry/heartbeat-aware: sweep_orphans() at boot blindly marks all running jobs interrupted because it assumes one just-died process. With N workers + rolling restarts that would kill healthy in-flight jobs. Use RQ's StartedJobRegistry / worker heartbeats (or Celery task-revocation + a periodic reaper) to mark only jobs whose worker is provably dead as interrupted, and reconcile DB running-rows against the live worker set rather than wiping on every boot.

Classifier cache across worker processes: each worker process keeps its own module-global catalog cache + all-MiniLM-L6-v2 matrix, rebuilt lazily when its DB-polled catalog_version() changes. This design already survives multi-process - no cross-process invalidation broadcast is required as long as every classify path calls _refresh_locked() (which reads catalog_version() from the shared DB). Cost: N copies of torch + the embedding model in RAM; the FastAPI web process should NOT import classification (segmentation-only), so only worker processes pay it. reset_catalog_cache() moves from the Flask app-factory hook to a worker-startup hook.

Provenance timing: catalog_revision/model/prompt_version are stamped at submit today. Carry them in the enqueue payload so the worker records the revision that was current when the user clicked, not whatever the catalog is when the job eventually runs - this keeps SegmentRow/Summary traceable to the exact catalog state, matching current behavior.

Nested CLASSIFY_WORKERS pool inside a worker: run_segmentation spawns its own ThreadPoolExecutor(4) per job. With external workers, total Vertex concurrency = (worker count) x (CLASSIFY_WORKERS) x windows - budget this against the shared quota; RQ worker count + CLASSIFY_WORKERS become the two concurrency knobs replacing PIPELINE_WORKERS. genai_retry backoff still applies inside each call.

Synchronous inline LLM routes (resummarize, bundle_summarize) currently block a Flask request thread. Under async FastAPI they must run in a threadpool (run_in_threadpool) or, preferably, be folded onto the same queue so they honor the one-active-job invariant and shared-quota backpressure instead of racing the background jobs.

SQLite is kept but is now written by web + N worker processes concurrently. Every process's engine must install the WAL/busy_timeout/foreign_keys pragmas from extensions.py on connect. WAL allows many readers + one writer; busy_timeout=5000 is the only backpressure - validate it holds under the new writer count or plan the documented ORM path to Postgres if OperationalError('database is locked') appears.

The classic path (services/jobs.py, state.py, review_api.py) is dropped, which is exactly what lets the target run multi-process; nothing in the document-scoped flow reads state.py, so removal is clean.
risks: PHI in the broker: Redis payloads and any RDB/AOF persistence could retain PHI if row text, extracted OCR text, or original_filename are enqueued. Mitigation is architectural - enqueue ONLY non-PHI identifiers/metadata (document_id UUID, kind, model, prompt_version, catalog_revision) and let workers read PHI from the DB/disk; still put Redis on the internal/BAA-covered network with TLS + auth and, ideally, disable persistence or encrypt at rest.

Vertex shared-quota storms: moving from 2 in-process threads to N independent worker processes multiplies concurrent generate_content calls (further multiplied by the inner CLASSIFY_WORKERS pool). Without a global concurrency cap this triggers 429 RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED storms that genai_retry backoff can only partially absorb; per-day/free-tier exhaustion is non-retryable and fails the job.

Losing the in-process _submit_lock allows a cross-process race where two enqueue calls both pass the active_job() check and create two active jobs for one document (double-execution, duplicate SegmentRows/Summaries, corrupted status). A DB unique-partial-index or distributed lock is mandatory, not optional.

Boot orphan-sweep mass-marking: if sweep_orphans() semantics are ported naively, any web/worker restart marks healthy in-flight jobs owned by still-alive workers as interrupted, silently killing long (minutes-to-hours) segmentation runs.

Cache coherence + memory across processes: N worker processes each hold a full sentence-transformers model + embedding matrix (RAM cost) and an independent catalog cache; a catalog edit is picked up per-process only on the next classify's revision poll, so concurrent jobs can legitimately run on different catalog revisions (recorded in provenance, acceptable) but operators may find the divergence surprising.

SQLite write contention: multiple worker processes writing throttled progress + bulk row/summary inserts against one SQLite file raises lock contention; exceeding busy_timeout surfaces as OperationalError('database is locked') mid-job. The 'keep SQLite' decision caps practical write concurrency.
openQuestions: RQ vs Celery for the broker: RQ is Redis-only and closely matches the current simple submit/poll model; Celery adds routing/retries/beat but more moving parts. Which fits the single-box + eventual-scale target?

Keep SQLite with N external worker processes, or is this the trigger for the documented Postgres move (config.py:35-37 says the ORM makes it a config change)? Depends on measured lock contention under real worker counts.

How to enforce one-active-job-per-document across processes: DB partial-unique index (portable, durable) vs Redis SETNX lock (fast, but a second source of truth) vs advisory lock - and how does the chosen mechanism map back to the existing 409 responses?

Progress storage: keep writing progress to the DB (current contract, survives broker restart) or use Redis job meta (less SQLite contention but a second read path for the status endpoint)? Task direction is DB - confirm.

Orphan/heartbeat strategy: rely on RQ StartedJobRegistry + worker heartbeat, Celery late-ack + visibility timeout, or a custom periodic reaper comparing jobs.state='running' against live workers? What heartbeat/timeout marks a job interrupted vs still-running?
Should the currently-synchronous resummarize and bundle_summarize move onto the queue (honoring the invariant + quota) or stay inline via FastAPI's threadpool?

Embedding-model footprint: accept N per-worker copies of all-MiniLM-L6-v2 + torch, or run a dedicated classification worker/pool (or a shared embedding microservice) so only some processes load torch?

Auth/session replacement: Flask-Security (CSRF cookie/header, registerable, session cookies) has no FastAPI equivalent - what auth model (JWT/session) backs the ownership (_own/IDOR) checks the job endpoints depend on?

Does the worker re-read catalog_version() at run time or trust the submit-time stamp for provenance? (Current code stamps at submit; confirm this is the intended semantics for delayed-start queued jobs.)

result.summary: The modern UI is a server-rendered-shell + vanilla-JS SPA-lite: Flask serves static HTML page shells (pages.py routes) whose behavior lives entirely in hand-written vanilla JS that talks to JSON APIs. There is no build step, no framework, no bundler. Two shared components carry most of the weight: window.MRR (the review-editor engine in review.js, mounted by both /review and the bundle pages) and the DocTable factory (doc-table.js, used by My Documents and both bundle pickers via _doc_table.html). Data-fetching is fetch() with an Accept: application/json header, a cookie-to-header XSRF pattern (X-XSRF-Token read from the XSRF-TOKEN cookie on unsafe methods), and 401->redirect-to-/login. Job progress is surfaced by two polling loops: doc-table.js polls the list every 2s while any doc has an active_job, and review.js polls /status every 1s during segment/summarize. The Evaluators design system (evaluators.css) is a CSS-custom-property token set plus ev-*/hd-*/rc-*/sum-*/auth-* component classes; a React UI must reproduce these tokens as CSS vars or a Tailwind theme. Auth pages (login/register) are Flask-Security-Too WTForms renders extending security/base.html. The classic pages (index.html + 7 others, served under /classic, /pages, etc.) are the jQuery/select2 single-user monolith UI backed by state.py globals - drop. Every modern page/component maps to a rewrite in React/Next; the API contracts they consume are stable and can be reused as-is by the FastAPI back-end.

files.role: Shared review-editor engine exposed as window.MRR (identify/review-correct/summaries flow, autosave, PDF.js viewer sync, export dialog); driven by /review and both bundle pages
DocTable factory: status badges, filter chips, filename search, sortable headers, pagination, per-row overflow menu, 2s active-job polling; reused by My Documents and bundle pickers

Evaluators design system: :root CSS-custom-property tokens (navy/gold/blue/gray scales, semantic, shadows, radii, fluid --gutter/--content-cap, Poppins/Inter fonts) + all ev-*/hd-*/rc-*/sum-*/auth-* component classes
My Documents page shell (topbar nav, empty-state dropzone, includes _doc_table.html, hidden file input)
My Documents logic: DocTable wiring, upload (button/browse/drag-drop), empty-vs-populated toggle
Review page shell: topbar, 3-step ev-stepper, includes _doc_editor.html, summaries section, ev-dialog export modal; <body data-doc-id> triggers MRR auto-boot
Shared identify/progress/review-correct editor markup (rows table + PDF iframe split), included by review.html and bundle.html
Shared documents-table markup (toolbar + sortable table + pager) driven by doc-table.js
Bundle workspace shell (Diagnostic&Operative / Depositions); data-bundle-* attrs pass label/slug/categories; loads review.js + doc-table.js + bundle-page.js
Bundle logic: picker(DocTable)->mount shared MRR editor in place, category filter injection, extract-PDF / summarize-to-Word of matching-category docs

Admin CRUD over /api/admin (category list/create/patch/toggle, prompt get/put)
